title: Changing Directories: `cd`
description: Master the art of moving through the filesystem using absolute and relative paths.

slides.title: The Teleporter: `cd` 🌀
slides.content:
# Change Directory

Knowing where you are is useless if you can't go anywhere else.

The command is `cd` (Change Directory).

**Usage:**
```bash
cd [destination]
```

### Example:
```bash
cd /home/cybron/Documents
```

You are now standing in the `Documents` folder. Run `pwd` to prove it to yourself.

quizData.question: What command moves you from `/home/user` to `/home/user/Downloads`?
quizData.explanation: Since you are already in `/home/user`, you can just type the folder name `Downloads`.

slides.title: Absolute Paths: The Full Address 🗺️
slides.content:
# Starting from the Root

An **Absolute Path** always starts with a `/`. It is the **complete** street address of a file or folder.

```bash
cd /usr/share/themes
```

No matter where you currently are in the filesystem, if you type this exact command, you will end up in `/usr/share/themes`.

### Analogy: GPS Coordinates 🌍
An absolute path is like giving someone Latitude and Longitude coordinates. It works no matter where they start.

slides.title: Relative Paths: Turn Left at the Big Tree ➡️
slides.content:
# Starting from Where You Stand

A **Relative Path** does **NOT** start with a `/`. It's a set of directions based on your *current* location.

Let's say you are in `/home/cybron`.

*   **Command:** `cd Documents`
*   **Result:** You go to `/home/cybron/Documents`.

### Analogy: Turn-by-Turn Directions 🚗
*   Absolute: "Go to 123 Main Street, Springfield."
*   Relative: "Walk out of this room, turn right, go to the end of the hall."

**Why use Relative Paths?** They are much shorter to type!

slides.title: ⚠️ Common Trap
slides.content:
## `cd /Documents` vs `cd Documents`

*   **`cd /Documents`**: Go to the **Root** of the drive and look for a folder called `Documents`. (This folder doesn't exist by default on Linux, so it will fail).
*   **`cd Documents`**: Look for a folder called `Documents` inside my **current** directory.

One tiny slash makes a world of difference!

quizData.question: Which of these is an Absolute Path?
quizData.explanation: Absolute paths always start with a forward slash `/` from the root directory.

slides.title: Going Back Home: `cd` with no arguments 🏠
slides.content:
# The Fastest Way Home

No matter how deep you wander into the filesystem tree...

`/usr/share/icons/Adwaita/96x96/emblems/`

...you can snap back to your safe **Home Directory** instantly by typing:

```bash
cd
```

That's it. Just `cd` with nothing after it. It's a magical teleport back to `/home/your_username`.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## `cd -` : The Undo Button

Want to go back to the *last* directory you were in?

```bash
cd -
```

This is like the 'Back' button in a web browser. It swaps you back and forth between two directories.
